meta.title: Drift — Sculptural Sound Art
meta.description: Drift reimagines the white noise machine as sculptural sound art — exploring the history of sonic self-control from Buckwalter's first noise machine to the present, and asking what we lose when we mask the silence.

header.title: Drift
header.subtitle: Sculptural sound art exploring the continuum between noise and music.

hero.title: The white noise machine, reimagined as art.
hero.introCopy: For decades, people have built technologies to control what they hear — white noise machines to sleep, nature recordings to focus, noise-cancelling headphones to tune out. Drift takes this impulse and makes it visible — and monumental. Scaled larger than a human, these sculptures reimagine the white noise machine as an object you confront rather than ignore. Using an Arduino microcontroller, servo motors, wood and tuned pipes, each sculpture slowly shifts from noise toward emergent musical patterns — inviting audiences to notice the sonic self-control they practice every day.

noteCards.title: What you'll experience
noteCards.body: A dynamic soundscape among sculptures incorporating resonant materials that reflect both light and sound. Witness the transition from masking noise to emergent music.

noteCards.title: An interdisciplinary collaboration
noteCards.body: Sound design and programming by Tate Carson, design and fabrication by Tim Murray. The project integrates electronics and software with traditional woodcraft.

noteCards.title: The central question
noteCards.body: What do we lose when we paper over silence? Drift asks audiences to sit with the sounds they ordinarily mask — and to notice what emerges.

process.title: How the work came together.
process.description: Drift evolved through repeated cycles of prototyping, material testing, and sonic calibration. Each revision asked the same question: how can the sculpture remain visibly constructed — tool marks, seams, and all — while producing a transformed listening experience?

theory.title: How noise, sound, and listening shape daily life.
bulletList: Orphic Media: technologies like white noise machines modify perception and reshape mood and attention — what Mack Hagood calls orphic media — in ways we rarely examine.

Sonic self-control: from Buckwalter's first white noise machine to Bose's noise-cancelling headphones, people have invented tools to command their auditory environment. Drift makes this history audible.

Technologies of the self: Irv Teibel's Environments records promised not just masking noise but improving productivity, sleep, even plant growth — sound as a technology for coping with the pressures of modern life.

cards.title: Sonic self-control
cards.body: From a broken fan repurposed as the first white noise machine to headphones that subtract the world's frequencies, people have spent decades engineering their own silence. Drift enlarges that impulse into sculpture — making the pursuit of sonic control something you can see, hear, and stand beside.

cards.title: Noise as creative material
cards.body: The sculptures position noise not as something to mask or eliminate, but as creative material. Where a white noise machine says 'you don't listen to this,' Drift says 'listen closer.'

cards.title: What silence reveals
cards.body: Tinnitus is sometimes described as the brain's signal for silence — our attention straining to find meaning in the quiet. Drift asks a similar question: what emerges when we stop masking and start listening?

quote.text: We spend our lives controlling what we hear — masking, cancelling, curating. Drift asks what happens when we stop managing the soundscape and start listening to it.

footer.text: Drift will debut at DSU Research Week, Dakota State University, Madison, SD. The project is supported by DSU and integrates sound art scholarship with innovative artistic methods.
